perturbation.original_text: The first paid (Initial) Maintenance Term shall commence upon expiration of the Warranty Period and shall continue for twelve (12) months thereafter. Thereafter, the Maintenance Term shall automatically renew for successive period, 12 months, on the terms and conditions of this Agreement unless Bank of America terminates Maintenance Services pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The first paid (Initial) Maintenance Term shall commence upon expiration of the Warranty Period and shall continue for twelve (12) months thereafter. Thereafter, the Maintenance Term shall automatically renew at Supplier's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By removing Bank of America's right to terminate maintenance services and allowing renewal at only the supplier's discretion, the contract becomes unfair and potentially unconscionable, especially if the services are substandard or no longer needed. This is a violation of general contract law principles. This also could violate California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a)(5) states that it is unlawful to represent that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities which they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection which he or she does not have. By removing the customer's ability to cancel services, the supplier might be representing that the services have benefits to the customer that they no longer do.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1770 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The modification removes Bank of America's right to terminate maintenance services, granting renewal solely at the supplier's discretion. This directly contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a), which deems unfair methods of competition and deceptive practices unlawful when selling goods or services to consumers. Specifically, 1770(a)(5) prohibits representing that services have benefits they do not have. By eliminating the customer's termination right, the supplier potentially misrepresents the service's benefits, as the customer is locked in regardless of service quality or need. The original contract allowed termination, providing a benefit absent in the modified version.

perturbation.original_text: Bank of America may terminate Maintenance Services for convenience at any time in accordance with the Section entitled "Termination" of the Agreement. If Bank of America terminates the Maintenance Services, Bank of America shall have the right to reinstate the Maintenance Services without paying any reinstatement fee.
perturbation.changed_text: Bank of America may terminate Maintenance Services for convenience at any time in accordance with the Section entitled "Termination" of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that Bank of America can reinstate the maintenance services without a fee introduces an imbalance of power. If the service is critical, forcing them to pay a reinstatement fee could be considered unconscionable, especially if termination was due to a minor issue or if the fee is excessive.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Com. Code § 2302 states that if the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. Removing the right to reinstate without a fee and therefore forcing a new fee can be determined as unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2302 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a clause that allowed Bank of America to reinstate maintenance services without a fee. The Law Explanation argues that forcing a new fee could be deemed unconscionable under Cal. Com. Code § 2302. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to California Code, COM 2302, supporting the claim that such a provision falls under California's commercial code, which may render the changed contract unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: The period commencing on the Installation Date and continuing for the number of days specified: 120 days
perturbation.changed_text: The period commencing on the Installation Date and continuing for the number of days specified: 30 days
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the acceptance period from 120 days to 30 days might violate the implied warranty of merchantability under California law if the software is complex and requires a longer period to reasonably assess its functionality and suitability. This drastically reduced acceptance period could be seen as unfair, especially considering the Bank of America's dependence on its functionality for critical business processes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Com. Code § 2314 states the Implied Warranty: Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Reducing the acceptance period may not give the company sufficient time to evaluate this warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 120 days for the acceptance period, while the changed text reduces it to 30 days. The Law Explanation states Cal. Com. Code § 2314, which is provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies a warranty of merchantability. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this reduction might violate this implied warranty, particularly for complex software requiring a longer evaluation period. The scraped law snippet 1 supports that unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Therefore, the reduced acceptance period could be argued as insufficient time to evaluate the software's merchantability, creating a contradiction. The "Omission Legal Contradiction" perturbation type confirms that the contract now omits a legally mandated consumer protection.
